(September 26, 1930 – December 3, 2018) was an American [actor](/wiki/Actor "Actor"). He was known for his [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award")-winning performance as Saunders in the 1989 Broadway production of *[Lend Me a Tenor](/wiki/Lend_Me_a_Tenor "Lend Me a Tenor")*, and for his starring role in the 2007 film *[The Savages](/wiki/The_Savages_(film) "The Savages (film)")*. Bosco won a [Daytime Emmy Award](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Awards "Daytime Emmy Awards") in 1988.

**Philip Michael Bosco** (September 26, 1930 – December 3, 2018) was an American [actor](/wiki/Actor "Actor"). He was known for his [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award")-winning performance as Saunders in the 1989 Broadway production of *[Lend Me a Tenor](/wiki/Lend_Me_a_Tenor "Lend Me a Tenor")*, and for his starring role in the 2007 film *[The Savages](/wiki/The_Savages_(film) "The Savages (film)")*. Bosco won a [Daytime Emmy Award](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Awards "Daytime Emmy Awards") in 1988.

## Life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Philip_Bosco&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and career")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Philip_Bosco&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

Philip Michael Bosco was born in [Jersey City, New Jersey](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), on September 26, 1930, to Margaret Raymond (née Thek), a policewoman, and Philip Lupo Bosco, a carnival worker.[[1]](#cite_note-filmr-1) His father was of Italian descent and his mother was of German descent.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Bosco attended [St. Peter's Preparatory School](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Preparatory_School "St. Peter's Preparatory School") in Jersey City,[[3]](#cite_note-Record2007-3) and later studied drama at [Catholic University of America](/wiki/The_Catholic_University_of_America "The Catholic University of America"), where he had notable success in the [title role](/wiki/King_Richard_III_of_England "King Richard III of England") of [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s *[Richard III](/wiki/Richard_III_(play) "Richard III (play)")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Philip_Bosco&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Career")]

Bosco began his career in [Broadway theatre](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") and earned a Tony Award nomination for his debut in *The Rape of the Belt* in 1960. Bosco spent the next three decades supporting major stars in classic revivals like *[Cyrano de Bergerac](/wiki/Cyrano_de_Bergerac_(play) "Cyrano de Bergerac (play)")*, *[King Lear](/wiki/King_Lear "King Lear"),* and *[Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night "Twelfth Night")*.[[5]](#cite_note-ibdb-5)

Bosco appeared in revivals of plays by [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw"), including *[Man and Superman](/wiki/Man_and_Superman "Man and Superman")*, *[Saint Joan](/wiki/Saint_Joan_(play) "Saint Joan (play)")*, *[Mrs. Warren's Profession](/wiki/Mrs._Warren%27s_Profession "Mrs. Warren's Profession")*, *[Major Barbara](/wiki/Major_Barbara "Major Barbara")*, *[Heartbreak House](/wiki/Heartbreak_House "Heartbreak House")* (opposite [Rex Harrison](/wiki/Rex_Harrison "Rex Harrison")), and *[You Never Can Tell](/wiki/You_Never_Can_Tell_(play) "You Never Can Tell (play)")*, winning Tony nominations for the last three. Bosco also appeared with [Shirley Knight](/wiki/Shirley_Knight "Shirley Knight") in the [Roundabout Theatre Company](/wiki/Roundabout_Theatre_Company "Roundabout Theatre Company") revival of *[Come Back, Little Sheba](/wiki/Come_Back,_Little_Sheba_(play) "Come Back, Little Sheba (play)")*.[[5]](#cite_note-ibdb-5)

Following his Tony-winning performance in the farce *[Lend Me a Tenor](/wiki/Lend_Me_a_Tenor "Lend Me a Tenor")* in 1990, Bosco appeared on Broadway in *[An Inspector Calls](/wiki/An_Inspector_Calls "An Inspector Calls")* (1994), *[The Heiress](/wiki/The_Heiress_(1947_play) "The Heiress (1947 play)")* (1995), *[Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night "Twelfth Night")* (1998), *[Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen_(play) "Copenhagen (play)")* (2000), and *[Twelve Angry Men](/wiki/Twelve_Angry_Men_(1964_play) "Twelve Angry Men (1964 play)")* (2004).[[5]](#cite_note-ibdb-5)

Bosco played Grandpa Potts in the 2005 Broadway production of *[Chitty Chitty Bang Bang](/wiki/Chitty_Chitty_Bang_Bang_(musical) "Chitty Chitty Bang Bang (musical)")*, and played the aged Captain Shotover in a Broadway revival of *[Heartbreak House](/wiki/Heartbreak_House "Heartbreak House")* in 2006. He retired from the stage in 2009 after appearing in the City Center Encores production of *[Finian's Rainbow](/wiki/Finian%27s_Rainbow "Finian's Rainbow")*, although Bosco lent his voice to [Douglas Carter Beane](/wiki/Douglas_Carter_Beane "Douglas Carter Beane")'s 2010 play *Mr. and Mrs. Fitch*.[[5]](#cite_note-ibdb-5)

Bosco appeared regularly in the *[Law & Order](/wiki/Law_%26_Order "Law & Order")* television franchise, in roles ranging from judges to lawyers to villains. His motion picture credits include *[Trading Places](/wiki/Trading_Places "Trading Places")*, *[Working Girl](/wiki/Working_Girl "Working Girl")*, *[Children of a Lesser God](/wiki/Children_of_a_Lesser_God_(film) "Children of a Lesser God (film)")*, *[Suspect](/wiki/Suspect_(1987_film) "Suspect (1987 film)")*, *[Walls of Glass](/wiki/Walls_of_Glass_(film) "Walls of Glass (film)")*, *[Straight Talk](/wiki/Straight_Talk "Straight Talk")*, *[Nobody's Fool](/wiki/Nobody%27s_Fool_(1994_film) "Nobody's Fool (1994 film)")*, *[Wonder Boys](/wiki/Wonder_Boys_(film) "Wonder Boys (film)")*, *[The Money Pit](/wiki/The_Money_Pit "The Money Pit")*, *[Three Men and a Baby](/wiki/Three_Men_and_a_Baby "Three Men and a Baby")*, *[Milk Money](/wiki/Milk_Money_(film) "Milk Money (film)")*, *[Quick Change](/wiki/Quick_Change "Quick Change")*, *[Angie](/wiki/Angie_(1994_film) "Angie (1994 film)")*, *[The First Wives Club](/wiki/The_First_Wives_Club "The First Wives Club")*, and *[The Savages](/wiki/The_Savages_(film) "The Savages (film)")*.

Bosco narrated [Ric Burns](/wiki/Ric_Burns "Ric Burns")' 1991 documentary film *[Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island_(1991_film) "Coney Island (1991 film)")*, and voiced a number of characters for [Ken Burns](/wiki/Ken_Burns "Ken Burns")' documentaries for [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS"). He portrayed Vincenzo the butler in the 1995 comedy *[It Takes Two](/wiki/It_Takes_Two_(1995_film) "It Takes Two (1995 film)")*; and Walter Wallace, father of the bride-to-be, in the 1997 romantic comedy *[My Best Friend's Wedding](/wiki/My_Best_Friend%27s_Wedding "My Best Friend's Wedding")*, co-starring [Julia Roberts](/wiki/Julia_Roberts "Julia Roberts"), [Cameron Diaz](/wiki/Cameron_Diaz "Cameron Diaz") and [Dermot Mulroney](/wiki/Dermot_Mulroney "Dermot Mulroney").

In 1988, Bosco won a [Daytime Emmy Award](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Award "Daytime Emmy Award") for his appearance in the *[ABC Afterschool Special](/wiki/ABC_Afterschool_Special "ABC Afterschool Special")* "Read Between The Lines". He was a series regular on the [FX](/wiki/FX_Network "FX Network") original series *[Damages](/wiki/Damages_(TV_series) "Damages (TV series)")*. Bosco narrated *Desert Giant: The World of the Saguaro Cactus* by Barbara Bash on the [PBS](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service") series *[Reading Rainbow](/wiki/Reading_Rainbow "Reading Rainbow")* in its sixty-second episode on March 27, 1990.

Bosco was inducted into the [American Theater Hall of Fame](/wiki/American_Theater_Hall_of_Fame "American Theater Hall of Fame") in 1998.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Philip_Bosco&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Bosco married a fellow [Catholic University](/wiki/Catholic_University "Catholic University") student, Nancy Ann Dunkle, on January 2, 1957. They had seven children and 15 grandchildren.[[1]](#cite_note-filmr-1)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Bosco and his wife resided in [Haworth, New Jersey](/wiki/Haworth,_New_Jersey "Haworth, New Jersey").[[3]](#cite_note-Record2007-3)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On December 3, 2018, Bosco died at his home of complications from [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") at age 88.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
